subject: Human Resources
title: Henry Ford's Five-Dollar Day
description: A quick, practical guide to Henry Ford's Five-Dollar Day.

hook.text: In 1914, Henry Ford doubled his workers' pay to $5 a day. The business world called him insane.

buildup.text: Ford's assembly lines had brutal turnover—370% annually. Workers quit as fast as they were hired.

discovery.text: At $5 a day, turnover plummeted. Workers lined up for jobs. Productivity and quality soared.

twist.text: Ford's real genius: well-paid workers could afford to buy the cars they made—creating his own customers.

climax.text: The $5 day proved that fair pay isn't just an expense—it's an investment with measurable returns.

punchline.text: Ford paid more and got more. The math still works.

quiz.question: Why did Henry Ford double his workers' pay?
options: To reduce extreme turnover and boost productivity
